text:
Adverse Event Report AER-001
Date of event: 2025-05-06
Severity: serious
Department: ICU

SUMMARY
A medication error occurred in the ICU involving the medication warfarin. The event was first identified by a respiratory therapist during routine patient assessment. Initial investigation indicates the event was classified as serious.

NARRATIVE
The patient was receiving warfarin per the standing order when the respiratory therapist noted a discrepancy between the charted dose and the medication administration record. The respiratory therapist escalated the concern to the attending physician. Standard universal protocol time out steps were reviewed. No permanent harm was identified; the patient remained stable throughout the shift.

otocol time out steps were reviewed. No permanent harm was identified; the patient remained stable throughout the shift.

CONTRIBUTING FACTORS
- inadequate staffing
- inadequate staffing

FOLLOW-UP
Case referred for RCA review. Warfarin administration workflow in the ICU will be re-audited within 30 days.

Formulary Entry FORM-001: Heparin

DRUG CLASS
Refer to hospital pharmacopeia for heparin classification.

INDICATIONS
Standard indications per current hospital formulary.

MONITORING PARAMETERS
- Baseline labs before initiation
- Trough levels as indicated
- Re-evaluation of dose every 24-48 hours

HIGH-ALERT STATUS
Heparin is flagged as a high-alert medication. Independent double-check is required before administration in high-acuity settings such as the ICU and ER.

RELATED PROTOCOLS
- Handoff Sbar
- Medication Reconciliation

Protocol PROT-001: Medication Reconciliation

PURPOSE
Defines the standard steps for medication reconciliation in the PEDIATRICS and across any unit performing equivalent workflows.

SCOPE
Applies to all respiratory therapist and respiratory therapist staff, including temporary and float staff.

PROCEDURE
1. Verify patient identity with two identifiers.
2. Complete pre-procedure checklist as defined by the medication reconciliation bundle.
3. Perform required cross-checks before administration or intervention.
4. Document completion in the electronic health record within 15 minutes.

REFERENCES
- Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goals
- Internal policy manual, section on medication reconciliation.

Root Cause Analysis RCA-001
Incident reviewed: dosage error in the ONCOLOGY.
Involved medication (where applicable): insulin.

METHODOLOGY
A multidisciplinary RCA team convened within 72 hours of the event. The team applied the five whys technique and mapped the event timeline against the infection prevention bundle protocol.

ROOT CAUSES IDENTIFIED
- miscommunication handoff
- knowledge gap
- protocol deviation

CONTRIBUTING FACTORS
- Variability in ONCOLOGY staff-to-patient ratios during night shift
- Incomplete handoff from prior shift

ACTIONS
- Reinforce fall prevention bundle training across ONCOLOGY.
- Update medication reconciliation workflow for insulin.
- Introduce structured handoff using SBAR for shift changes.
